rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the row itself (climate_screen=explicit_self). The text commits to waste-heat recovery planning: 'heat waste locations will be identified, and actions to utilise this heat waste will be planned' and evaluates 'utilisation of sewage heating in properties', which is a direct mitigation lever by reducing primary heating energy demand and emissions. The gate failure ('no Mitigation outcome token found') reflects tokenization/ontology strictness, not lack of mitigation content. Primary category 'Mitigation' fits; no reclassification needed.
policy_statement: Identify major waste-heat locations and plan actions to utilise waste heat, including analysing impacts of property sewage heating on the wastewater treatment plant.
mechanism_description: The city will identify major sources of waste heat and plan actions to use that heat (including sewage heating), reducing the need for conventional heating energy while analysing effects on the wastewater treatment plant.
primary_causal_pathway: Same heating service → substitution with recovered waste heat → ↓ fossil/energy-system heat production → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in system/activity: The city plans and enables infrastructure/operations to capture waste heat (including sewage heat via heat exchangers/heat pumps and heat networks) and deliver it for building/process heating. (2) Mediating physical process: Thermal energy that would have been discharged to the environment is recovered and upgraded/distributed, displacing heat that would otherwise be generated by boilers/CHP or electric resistance heating; wastewater treatment impacts are assessed to avoid undermining plant performance. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net energy input and reduced fuel combustion and/or electricity demand for heating, resulting in reduced CO₂ (and potentially other) emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced combustion or grid electricity use for heating by substituting recovered waste heat, leading to lower GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Provide the same heating service with less conventional energy by substituting recovered waste heat, which primarily reduces GHG emissions from heat generation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism improves energy productivity (a resource-efficiency aspect), its principal climate significance across implementations is emissions reduction from displaced fossil/grid heating—therefore Mitigation is the most appropriate primary category.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the recovered heat is upgraded using high-carbon electricity, net mitigation could be smaller; however, the intended and typical dominant pathway remains displacement of higher-emission heating. Wastewater treatment operational considerations do not change the dominant climate pathway.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances of this mechanism: Any policy centered on planning/implementing waste-heat recovery for heating (including sewage heat) has the same primary effect of reducing conventional heating energy demand and associated emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any finer classification (e.g., district energy vs. building-level heat pumps, degree of electrification, or grid-carbon dependence) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG benefit can vary with the electricity carbon intensity for heat pumps and with what heating source is displaced (coal/gas vs. low-carbon district heat). In some contexts, recovered sewage heat could slightly increase wastewater treatment energy needs or affect treatment performance; these are typically managed constraints rather than the primary climate outcome.
key_indicators: “will be identified”; “actions to utilise this heat waste will be planned”; “impact… will be analysed”; “utilisation of sewage heating in properties”
co_benefits: Potential improved energy efficiency/cost savings; potential reduced thermal pollution; wastewater-system performance management
instance_edge_case_notes: No direct mandate, funding, or build-out is specified—this is upstream mapping/analysis and planning (enabling), though it targets a clearly mitigation-relevant heat-substitution pathway.
